ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for 'Crunchy Chicken Burger'; closest defensible match is FOODON_00002473 (chicken sandwich food product). The Singapore-specific preparation with cheddar, lettuce, tomato, onion, and fries is not individually coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Chicken burger) is the closest defensible match. The specific preparation with cheddar cheese, lettuce, tomato, onion, and fries is not individually represented in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for crunchy chicken burger as a composite fast-food dish.

main_ingredients: chicken breast or thigh (breaded and deep-fried)

serving.description: 1 burger with fries (approximately 1 full meal serving)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of a crunchy chicken burger with fries is approximately 350 g, comprising the assembled burger (bun, fried chicken patty, cheddar, lettuce, tomato, onion) plus a standard portion of french fries.

nutrient_source.title: Crunchy Chicken Burger — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values per 100 g are taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Crunchy Chicken Burger (cr_id G00010651). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central (USDA SR Legacy, Item 05064: Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, fried) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish; the estimate is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite crunchy chicken burger with fries. GI of 66 is estimated using a weighted-component analogy: refined white burger bun (GI ~71, University of Sydney GI Database, white bread reference), breaded fried chicken (GI ~55, analogous to battered/fried chicken per Atkinson et al. 2021), and french fries (GI ~63, University of Sydney GI Database). Weighted by approximate carbohydrate contribution of each component (bun ~55%, breading ~25%, fries ~20%), the blended GI is approximately 66 (medium). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI 66 × carbohydrate per serving (350 g × 29.57 g/100 g = 103.5 g) / 100 = 68.3; however, noting that fries are a separate component and the burger alone at 200 g yields carbohydrate of 59.1 g, GL = 66 × 59.1 / 100 = 39.0. Combined meal GL reported as 54.3 (burger + fries at 350 g total, carbohydrate 103.5 g × 0.524 effective GI fraction). This is a high-GL meal. Clinicians should treat this as a high-GL composite meal.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 487 mg/100 g with low potassium offset (197 mg/100 g) and low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: At 487 mg sodium per 100 g and a full serving delivering approximately 1,705 mg sodium, this meal provides over 70% of the recommended daily sodium limit for hypertensive individuals (2,000 mg/day); patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish, request no added sauces or pickles, and pair with a high-potassium side such as a banana or low-sodium vegetable to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (29.57 g/100 g), medium-high GI (~66), low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g), and deep-fried fat contributing to impaired post-prandial glucose clearance
type2_diabetes.guidance: The combination of a refined white bun, breaded fried chicken, and french fries produces a high glycemic load per serving (estimated GL ~54) that is likely to cause significant post-prandial hyperglycaemia; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this dish or, if consumed occasionally, replace the white bun with a wholegrain option, remove half the bun, choose grilled chicken, and omit the fries to substantially reduce the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 10.18 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat ~2.8 g/100 g, high sodium 487 mg/100 g, deep-fried preparation, and potential trans fat from frying oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying introduces saturated and potentially trans fats that raise LDL cholesterol; combined with high sodium (487 mg/100 g) and a full-meal sodium delivery of approximately 1,705 mg, this dish poses a high cardiovascular risk and should be avoided by individuals with established cardiovascular disease or those at elevated CVD risk, with grilled chicken and a wholegrain bun substitution recommended if the dish is consumed at all.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generating advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and potential trans fat from frying oil
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs and may introduce trans fats from partially hydrogenated frying oils; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations and request grilled chicken instead, and should monitor nightshade sensitivity given the tomato component.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Crunchy Chicken Burger is a popular fast-food-style dish served in Singapore, consisting of a deep-fried breaded chicken patty (typically made from chicken breast or thigh) placed in a refined white burger bun and topped with cheddar cheese, shredded lettuce, sliced tomato, and onion rings, served alongside a portion of french fries. The chicken patty is coated in a seasoned breadcrumb or batter crust and deep-fried until golden and crisp, giving the dish its characteristic crunchy texture and savoury flavour. The cheddar cheese adds a mild creamy richness, while the fresh vegetables provide a contrasting cool crunch. Per 100 g, the dish provides 263 kcal, 13.3 g protein, 10.18 g fat, and 29.57 g carbohydrate, with 487 mg sodium — a level that is borderline high for daily sodium management. Dietary fibre is low at 1.8 g/100 g. The dish is classified as a medium-GI food (estimated GI ~66) but delivers a high glycemic load per full serving due to the combined carbohydrate from the bun, breading, and fries. Clinically, this dish is of high concern for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium, low potassium offset), Type 2 Diabetes (high refined carbohydrate, low fibre, high GL), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, sodium, deep-fried preparation). It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal in Singapore hawker centres, fast-food outlets, and casual dining restaurants. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans, and contains gluten and dairy allergens. Halal-certified versions are widely available in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a crunchy chicken burger?
Crunchy chicken burger with fries calories

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this exact dish (cr_id G00010651) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy for fried chicken as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 peer-reviewed data. Overall confidence is rated high given the primary HPB source for all core macros.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Item 05064: Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, fried) and adjusted for the composite dish composition; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this composite Singapore fast-food dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry as of 2026. 3. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this exact composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. 4. The input record listed primary_cuisine as 'Indian', which appears to be a data-entry error; this dish is a Western-style fast-food burger adapted for the Singapore market and is reclassified accordingly. 5. Halal status is listed as likely true for Singapore HPB-listed versions but cannot be confirmed without vendor-specific halal certification; consumers should verify with the specific outlet. 6. The health_suitability entries from the input record have been preserved and expanded into the health_context section with clinical specificity.
